docs.title: Proxmox OpenAPI
docs.text: Schema-driven FastAPI package for Proxmox API: OpenAPI generation, mock data, and real API connections with validation.

docs.title: 🔄 Auto-Generated OpenAPI Schema
docs.text: Crawl the official Proxmox API Viewer and automatically generate complete OpenAPI 3.0 schemas with 646 operations across 428 endpoints.

docs.title: 🎭 Mock API Mode (Default)
docs.text:
Perfect for development and testing:

  • In-memory CRUD operations - Create, read, update, and delete mock Proxmox resources
  • Pre-generated 646 endpoints - Full Proxmox API surface ready to use
  • Custom mock data loading - Inject your own test data via JSON/YAML files
  • State persistence - Mock data persists across requests during runtime
  • No Proxmox server required - Test your code without a real Proxmox cluster

docs.title: 🔗 Real API Mode
docs.text:
Connect to actual Proxmox servers:

  • Full HTTP client with aiohttp - Efficient async connections
  • Request/response validation - Pydantic models ensure data integrity
  • Multiple auth methods - API tokens or username/password
  • SSL verification control - Flexible certificate handling
  • Production-ready - Battle-tested error handling and logging

docs.title: 📚 FastAPI Integration
docs.text:
• Automatic Swagger UI at /docs - Interactive API exploration
  • ReDoc documentation at /redoc - Clean, readable API docs
  • OpenAPI JSON at /openapi.json - Machine-readable schema

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: pip install proxmox-openapi

docs.title: Run Mock API (Default)
docs.text:
# Using the command-line tool
proxmox-openapi-mock

# Or with uvicorn directly
uvicorn proxmox_openapi.mock_main:app

Visit http://localhost:8000/docs to see 646 Proxmox API endpoints ready to use!

docs.title: Connect to Real Proxmox
docs.text:
# Set environment variables
export PROXMOX_API_MODE=real
export PROXMOX_API_URL=https://pve.example.com:8006
export PROXMOX_API_TOKEN_ID=user@pam!mytoken
export PROXMOX_API_TOKEN_SECRET=xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx

# Run the API
uvicorn proxmox_openapi.main:app

All requests now route to your real Proxmox server with full validation!

docs.title: For Developers
docs.text:
• Test Proxmox integrations without a real cluster
  • Prototype applications with full API mocking
  • CI/CD pipelines with consistent mock data
  • Local development without VPN/network access

docs.title: For DevOps/SRE
docs.text:
• API exploration with interactive Swagger docs
  • Schema validation for automation scripts
  • Custom tooling built on validated Proxmox operations
  • Production API proxy with request/response logging

docs.title: For QA/Testing
docs.text:
• Automated testing with reproducible mock data
  • Integration tests without environment dependencies
  • Load testing against mock endpoints
  • Regression testing with versioned schemas

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[Client] --> B{Proxmox OpenAPI}
    B -->|Mock Mode| C[In-Memory CRUD]
    B -->|Real Mode| D[ProxmoxClient]
    D --> E[Real Proxmox Server]
    C --> F[Generated Mock Data]
    D --> G[Request Validation]
    G --> D
    D --> H[Response Validation]
    H --> A

Mock Mode: Requests → Generated Endpoints → In-Memory State → Mock Response

Real Mode: Requests → Validation → aiohttp Client → Proxmox API → Validation → Response

docs.title: Example: Create a VM (Mock Mode)
docs.text:
import httpx

# POST to create a VM
response = httpx.post(
    "http://localhost:8000/api2/json/nodes/pve/qemu",
    json={
        "vmid": 100,
        "name": "test-vm",
        "memory": 2048,
        "cores": 2,
    }
)

print(response.json())
# {'vmid': 100, 'name': 'test-vm', 'memory': 2048, 'cores': 2, ...}

# GET to retrieve the VM
response = httpx.get("http://localhost:8000/api2/json/nodes/pve/qemu/100")
print(response.json())
# Returns the same VM data - state persisted!

docs.title: Example: Real Proxmox Connection
docs.text:
import os
from proxmox_openapi.main import create_app

# Configure real API connection
os.environ["PROXMOX_API_MODE"] = "real"
os.environ["PROXMOX_API_URL"] = "https://pve.example.com:8006"
os.environ["PROXMOX_API_TOKEN_ID"] = "user@pam!mytoken"
os.environ["PROXMOX_API_TOKEN_SECRET"] = "your-secret"

# Create app in real mode
app = create_app()

# Now all requests route to your real Proxmox server
# with full Pydantic validation on requests and responses!

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text: This page provides a comprehensive reference of all available endpoints in the Proxmox OpenAPI server.

docs.title: Endpoint Overview
docs.text:
The server provides 646 Proxmox VE API endpoints organized into the following categories:

  • Access Control - Users, groups, roles, permissions, ACLs
  • Cluster - Cluster configuration, HA, resources, firewall
  • Nodes - Node management, services, tasks, storage
  • Storage - Storage configuration and content management
  • Virtual Machines (VMs) - VM lifecycle, configuration, snapshots
  • Containers (LXC) - Container lifecycle, configuration, snapshots
  • Network - SDN, VLANs, zones, subnets
  • Pools - Resource pools
  • Version - API version information

docs.title: Interactive Documentation
docs.text:
The best way to explore the API is through the interactive Swagger UI included with the server:

# Start the server
uvicorn proxmox_openapi.main:app --reload

# Open in browser
http://localhost:8000/docs

The Swagger UI provides:

  • Complete endpoint list with descriptions
  • Request/response schemas for all operations
  • Try-it-out functionality to test endpoints
  • Example request/response payloads
  • Authentication configuration

docs.title: Version Information
docs.text:
GET /version

Returns Proxmox VE API version information.

Response: 

{
  "version": "8.1",
  "release": "1",
  "repoid": "abc123"
}

docs.title: Access Tickets (Authentication)
docs.text:
POST /access/ticket

Create authentication ticket (username/password login).

Request Body: 

{
  "username": "root@pam",

}

Response: 

{
  "ticket": "PVE:root@pam:...",
  "CSRFPreventionToken": "...",

}

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
Most endpoints require authentication. See the Real API Guide for details on:

  • API token authentication
  • Username/password authentication
  • Setting up credentials

docs.title: Response Codes
docs.text:
The API uses standard HTTP response codes:

Code Description 200 Success 201 Created 400 Bad Request - Invalid parameters 401 Unauthorized - Missing or invalid authentication 403 Forbidden - Insufficient permissions 404 Not Found - Resource doesn't exist 500 Internal Server Error

docs.title: Pagination
docs.text:
Some endpoints support pagination via query parameters:

  • limit - Maximum number of results (default: varies by endpoint)
  • start - Starting offset (default: 0)

Example: 

GET /nodes/pve1/qemu?limit=10&start=0

docs.title: Filtering
docs.text: Many list endpoints support filtering via query parameters. Check the Swagger UI for available filters per endpoint.

docs.title: Error Responses
docs.text:
Error responses follow this format:

{
  "detail": "Error message describing what went wrong",
  "errors": [
    {
      "field": "vmid",
      "message": "VM ID already exists"
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Schema Models
docs.text:
All request and response schemas are validated using Pydantic models. You can find the complete model definitions in:

  • Swagger UI - Interactive schema browser at /docs
  • ReDoc - Alternative documentation at /redoc
  • OpenAPI JSON - Raw schema at /openapi.json

docs.title: Mock vs Real Mode
docs.text: The behavior of endpoints differs based on the mode:

docs.title: Mock Mode (Default)
docs.text:
• In-memory CRUD operations
  • No actual Proxmox connection required
  • Data resets on server restart
  • Perfect for development and testing

docs.title: Real Mode
docs.text:
• Proxies requests to real Proxmox VE API
  • Requires valid Proxmox credentials
  • Full request/response validation
  • Production-ready

See the Mock API Guide and Real API Guide for mode-specific details.

docs.title: Rate Limiting
docs.text: Currently, no rate limiting is implemented. This may be added in future versions.

docs.title: Versioning
docs.text:
The API follows the Proxmox VE API versioning scheme. The current implementation is based on Proxmox VE 8.1.

For the most up-to-date endpoint reference, always check the Swagger UI at /docs when running the server.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: This document explains the internal architecture of the Proxmox OpenAPI project, including how the components work together and key design decisions.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Proxmox OpenAPI server is a dual-mode FastAPI application that can operate in two distinct modes:

  1. Mock Mode (Default) - Provides in-memory CRUD operations with pre-generated OpenAPI schema
  2. Real Mode - Acts as a validated proxy to a real Proxmox VE API
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              Proxmox OpenAPI Server                     │
│                                                         │
│  ┌──────────────────┐         ┌──────────────────┐    │
│  │   Mock Mode      │   OR    │   Real Mode      │    │
│  │                  │         │                  │    │
│  │  In-memory CRUD  │         │  Proxmox Proxy   │    │
│  │  646 endpoints   │         │  + Validation    │    │
│  └──────────────────┘         └──────────────────┘    │
│                                                         │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │        FastAPI Core + Swagger UI                │  │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Mock Mode
docs.text:
Mock mode loads pre-generated OpenAPI schemas and dynamically creates CRUD endpoints with in-memory state management.

┌───────────────┐
│  User Request │
└───────┬───────┘
        │
        ▼
┌───────────────────┐
│  FastAPI Router   │
└───────┬───────────┘
        │
        ▼
┌────────────────────────────┐
│  Generated Mock Endpoint   │  (created from OpenAPI schema)
│  - Validates request       │
│  - Performs CRUD operation │
│  - Returns response        │
└────────┬───────────────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌─────────────────────┐
│  MockState          │  (in-memory dictionary)
│  - Stores resources │
│  - Generates IDs    │
└─────────────────────┘

Key Components:

  • mock/routes.py - Route builder that reads OpenAPI schema and generates FastAPI endpoints
  • mock/state.py - In-memory state management with dict-based storage
  • mock/loader.py - Custom mock data loader from JSON/YAML files
  • schema.py - Schema loading utilities

Flow:

  1. App startup loads generated/proxmox/latest/openapi.json
  2. register_generated_proxmox_mock_routes() iterates through all paths/operations
  3. For each operation, creates a dynamic FastAPI route with:
  4. Path parameters from URL template
  5. Query parameters from schema
  6. Request body validation (Pydantic)
  7. Response model validation (Pydantic)
  8. Routes perform CRUD operations on MockState
  9. Data persists only in memory (resets on restart)

docs.title: Real Mode
docs.text:
Real mode proxies requests to an actual Proxmox VE API with full request/response validation.

┌───────────────┐
│  User Request │
└───────┬───────┘
        │
        ▼
┌───────────────────┐
│  FastAPI Router   │
└───────┬───────────┘
        │
        ▼
┌────────────────────────────┐
│  Real API Endpoint         │
│  - Validates request       │
│  - Calls ProxmoxClient     │
│  - Validates response      │
└────────┬───────────────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌─────────────────────┐
│  ProxmoxClient      │  (aiohttp)
│  - Authenticates    │
│  - Makes HTTP call  │
│  - Handles errors   │
└────────┬────────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌─────────────────────┐
│  Real Proxmox API   │
│  (8.1 or later)     │
└─────────────────────┘

Key Components:

  • proxmox/config.py - Configuration loading from environment variables
  • proxmox/client.py - aiohttp-based client with authentication
  • proxmox/routes.py - Route builder that proxies to real API
  • schema.py - Shared schema utilities

Flow:

  1. App startup loads ProxmoxConfig from environment
  2. register_proxmox_routes() creates endpoints similar to mock mode
  3. For each request:
  4. Validate request using Pydantic models
  5. Call ProxmoxClient.request()
  6. ProxmoxClient authenticates (API token or username/password)
  7. Forward request to real Proxmox API
  8. Validate response
  9. Return to user
  10. All data comes from real Proxmox server

docs.title: Code Generation Pipeline
docs.text:
The project includes a sophisticated code generation pipeline that converts the Proxmox VE API into OpenAPI schemas and Pydantic models.

┌──────────────────┐
│  Proxmox VE API  │
│  /api2/json/...  │
└────────┬─────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌──────────────────────┐
│  ProxmoxCrawler      │  (recursive API exploration)
│  - GET /api2/json    │
│  - Follow children   │
│  - Capture metadata  │
└────────┬─────────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌──────────────────────┐
│  raw_capture.json    │  (646 endpoints)
│  - Paths             │
│  - Methods           │
│  - Parameters        │
└────────┬─────────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌──────────────────────┐
│  Pipeline            │  (normalization)
│  - Deduplicate       │
│  - Enrich metadata   │
│  - Extract schemas   │
└────────┬─────────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌──────────────────────┐
│  OpenAPIBuilder      │  (schema generation)
│  - Create paths      │
│  - Build schemas     │
│  - Add security      │
└────────┬─────────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌──────────────────────┐
│  openapi.json        │  (5.2MB OpenAPI 3.1)
│  - 646 operations    │
│  - 428 paths         │
└────────┬─────────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌──────────────────────┐
│  PydanticBuilder     │  (model generation)
│  - Parse schemas     │
│  - Generate classes  │
│  - Add validators    │
└────────┬─────────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌──────────────────────┐
│  pydantic_models.py  │  (Python models)
│  - Request models    │
│  - Response models   │
└──────────────────────┘

Key Classes:

  • ProxmoxCrawler - Recursively explores Proxmox API structure
  • ProxmoxCodegenPipeline - Orchestrates the full pipeline
  • OpenAPIBuilder - Converts normalized data → OpenAPI schema
  • PydanticBuilder - Converts OpenAPI schema → Pydantic models

docs.title: Mock Mode Request Flow
docs.text:
User → FastAPI → validate_request() → mock_endpoint()
                                           │
                                           ▼
                                    MockState.get()
                                           │
                                           ▼
                                    validate_response()
                                           │
                                           ▼
                                    Return JSON

docs.title: Real Mode Request Flow
docs.text:
User → FastAPI → validate_request() → real_endpoint()
                                           │
                                           ▼
                                    ProxmoxClient
                                           │
                                           ▼
                                    auth_headers()
                                           │
                                           ▼
                                    aiohttp.request()
                                           │
                                           ▼
                                    Proxmox VE API
                                           │
                                           ▼
                                    validate_response()
                                           │
                                           ▼
                                    Return JSON

docs.title: 1. Dual-Mode Architecture
docs.text:
Why?

  • Development teams need mock APIs for testing without real infrastructure
  • Production deployments need validated proxies to real Proxmox servers
  • Single codebase reduces maintenance burden

Trade-offs:

  • ✅ Maximum flexibility for different use cases
  • ✅ Same API surface in both modes
  • ❌ More complex startup logic
  • ❌ Two code paths to maintain

docs.title: 2. Dynamic Route Generation
docs.text:
Why?

Instead of hardcoding 646 routes, we generate them from OpenAPI schema at runtime.

Benefits:

  • Schema updates automatically create new routes
  • No manual route maintenance
  • Guaranteed schema/implementation consistency
  • Smaller codebase

Trade-offs:

  • ✅ Extremely maintainable
  • ✅ Easy to update to new Proxmox versions
  • ❌ Slightly slower startup time (~1s)
  • ❌ More complex debugging

docs.title: 3. Pre-Generated Schemas
docs.text:
Why?

We ship pre-generated OpenAPI schemas instead of requiring live Proxmox access.

Benefits:

  • Users can run mock mode without any Proxmox server
  • Faster startup (no crawling needed)
  • Offline development support
  • Version pinning for reproducibility

Trade-offs:

  • ✅ Zero dependencies for mock mode
  • ✅ Works completely offline
  • ❌ Schema updates require regeneration
  • ❌ Larger repository size (~5MB per version)

docs.title: 4. Full Request/Response Validation
docs.text:
Why?

Every request and response passes through Pydantic validation.

Benefits:

  • Type safety for client applications
  • Early error detection
  • Self-documenting API via schemas
  • Swagger UI with accurate models

Trade-offs:

  • ✅ Production-quality reliability
  • ✅ Better developer experience
  • ❌ Small performance overhead
  • ❌ Stricter than raw Proxmox API

docs.title: 5. In-Memory Mock State
docs.text:
Why?

Mock mode uses a simple dict-based state instead of a database.

Benefits:

  • Zero dependencies (no database needed)
  • Fast CRUD operations
  • Simple testing
  • Easy to reset state

Trade-offs:

  • ✅ Extremely simple and fast
  • ✅ Perfect for testing/development
  • ❌ Data lost on restart
  • ❌ Not suitable for persistent mock environments

docs.title: 6. aiohttp for Real Mode
docs.text:
Why?

We chose aiohttp over httpx or requests.

Benefits:

  • Async/await native (matches FastAPI)
  • Battle-tested and mature
  • Excellent session/connection pooling
  • Good SSL/TLS handling

Trade-offs:

  • ✅ High performance
  • ✅ Native async integration
  • ❌ More complex than synchronous clients
  • ❌ Requires async context management

docs.title: Mock Mode
docs.text:
• Startup: ~1 second (schema loading + route generation)
  • Request latency: <5ms (in-memory operations)
  • Memory usage: ~100MB (schema + state)
  • Throughput: 10,000+ req/s (limited by FastAPI)

docs.title: Real Mode
docs.text:
• Startup: ~500ms (config loading + client setup)
  • Request latency: Proxmox latency + validation (~20-100ms overhead)
  • Memory usage: ~80MB (schema + client session)
  • Throughput: Limited by Proxmox server capacity

docs.title: Mock Mode
docs.text:
• No authentication by default (safe for development)
  • Can add custom auth middleware if needed
  • Intended for trusted development environments only

docs.title: Real Mode
docs.text:
• API Token authentication (recommended)
  • Username/Password authentication (fallback)
  • SSL/TLS verification (configurable)
  • All auth handled by ProxmoxClient
  • No credential storage in memory after initial auth

docs.title: Extension Points
docs.text:
The architecture is designed for easy extension:

  1. Custom Mock Data Loaders
  2. Implement load_custom_mock_data()

  3. Support new file formats (currently JSON/YAML)

  4. Custom Route Middleware

  5. Add FastAPI middleware for auth, logging, etc.

  6. Works with both mock and real modes

  7. New API Versions

  8. Run codegen pipeline with new Proxmox version
  9. Store in generated/proxmox/<version>/

  10. Switch via version parameter

  11. Alternative Backends

  12. Implement new mode (e.g., "database mode")
  13. Register routes conditionally in main.py

docs.title: Unit Tests
docs.text:
• test_schema.py - Schema loading and validation
  • test_mock_routes.py - Individual CRUD operations
  • test_proxmox_client.py - Client auth and requests (mocked)

docs.title: Integration Tests
docs.text:
• test_main_app.py - Full app startup in both modes
  • test_custom_mock_data.py - Data loading from files

docs.title: End-to-End Tests
docs.text:
• Manual testing via Swagger UI
  • Real Proxmox environment testing for real mode

docs.title: Future Architecture Considerations
docs.text:
Potential improvements for future versions:

  1. Database-Backed Mock Mode
  2. SQLite or PostgreSQL for persistent mock data

  3. Would enable multi-user testing scenarios

  4. Caching Layer

  5. Cache frequently-accessed Proxmox resources

  6. Reduce load on real Proxmox API

  7. WebSocket Support

  8. Real-time updates for task status

  9. Event streaming for cluster changes

  10. Plugin System

  11. Allow custom endpoint extensions

  12. Third-party Proxmox integrations

  13. GraphQL Layer

  14. Alternative query interface
  15. More flexible client queries

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text:
The Proxmox OpenAPI architecture balances:

  • Flexibility - Mock and real modes for different use cases
  • Simplicity - Minimal dependencies, straightforward code
  • Maintainability - Schema-driven, auto-generated routes
  • Reliability - Full validation, type safety, error handling

This design enables rapid development, comprehensive testing, and production-ready Proxmox API integration.

docs.title: main.py - Main Application
docs.text:
The central FastAPI app that supports both mock and real modes:

from fastapi import FastAPI
from proxmox_openapi import __version__
from proxmox_openapi.proxmox.config import ProxmoxConfig

config = ProxmoxConfig()
app = FastAPI(title="Proxmox OpenAPI", version=__version__)

if config.mode == "real":
    # Load real Proxmox routes
    from proxmox_openapi.proxmox.routes import register_proxmox_routes
    register_proxmox_routes(app, config)
else:
    # Load mock routes (default)
    from proxmox_openapi.mock.routes import register_generated_proxmox_mock_routes
    register_generated_proxmox_mock_routes(app, "latest")

docs.title: mock/routes.py - Mock Route Builder
docs.text:
Dynamically generates CRUD endpoints from OpenAPI schema:

def register_generated_proxmox_mock_routes(
    app: FastAPI,
    version: str = "latest",
    mock_data_path: str | None = None,
) -> None:
    """Register all mock routes from pre-generated schema."""
    # Load schema
    schema = load_proxmox_schema(version)

    # Create in-memory state
    state = MockState()

    # Load custom data if provided
    if mock_data_path:
        load_custom_mock_data(state, mock_data_path)

    # Generate routes for each endpoint
    for path, methods in schema["paths"].items():
        for method, operation in methods.items():
            create_mock_endpoint(app, state, method, path, operation)

docs.title: proxmox/client.py - Real API Client
docs.text:
aiohttp-based client with authentication and validation:

class ProxmoxClient:
    """Client for real Proxmox VE API."""

    async def request(
        self,
        method: str,
        path: str,
        params: dict | None = None,
        json: dict | None = None,
    ) -> dict:
        """Make authenticated request to Proxmox API."""
        # Build URL
        url = f"{self.config.url}/api2/json{path}"

        # Add auth headers
        headers = await self._get_auth_headers()

        # Make request
        async with self.session.request(
            method, url, headers=headers, params=params, json=json
        ) as response:
            response.raise_for_status()
            return await response.json()

docs.title: schema.py - Schema Utilities
docs.text:
Schema loading and validation:

def load_proxmox_schema(version: str = "latest") -> dict:
    """Load pre-generated Proxmox OpenAPI schema."""
    schema_path = (
        Path(__file__).parent
        / "generated"
        / "proxmox"
        / version
        / "openapi.json"
    )

    with open(schema_path) as f:
        return json.load(f)

docs.title: Code Generation Pipeline
docs.text: The project includes a powerful code generation pipeline that converts the Proxmox API into OpenAPI schemas:

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Proxmox VE API
      ↓
[1. Crawler] - Fetch API structure
      ↓
raw_capture.json (646 endpoints)
      ↓
[2. Pipeline] - Normalize & enrich
      ↓
normalized data
      ↓
[3. OpenAPI Builder] - Generate OpenAPI schema
      ↓
openapi.json (5.2MB)
      ↓
[4. Pydantic Builder] - Generate models
      ↓
pydantic_models.py

from proxmox_openapi.proxmox_codegen import ProxmoxCodegenPipeline

# Initialize pipeline
pipeline = ProxmoxCodegenPipeline(
    proxmox_url="https://proxmox.example.com:8006",
    username="root@pam",
    password="password",
    verify_ssl=False,
)

# Run full pipeline
await pipeline.run_full_pipeline(
    output_dir="./output",
    version_tag="8.1",

import pytest
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
from proxmox_openapi.main import app

def test_version_endpoint():

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_proxmox_client_auth(mock_proxmox_server):
    """Test ProxmoxClient authentication."""
    from proxmox_openapi.proxmox import ProxmoxClient, ProxmoxConfig

    config = ProxmoxConfig(
        url=mock_proxmox_server.url,
        api_token="test-token",

    client = ProxmoxClient(config)
    result = await client.request("GET", "/version")

docs.title: FAQ
docs.text: Frequently Asked Questions about the Proxmox OpenAPI server.

docs.title: What is Proxmox OpenAPI?
docs.text:
Proxmox OpenAPI is a FastAPI-based server that provides two modes:

  1. Mock Mode - In-memory Proxmox VE API simulator with 646 endpoints for development and testing
  2. Real Mode - Validated proxy to a real Proxmox VE API with full request/response validation

It's designed to help developers build, test, and integrate with Proxmox infrastructure without requiring a live Proxmox cluster for every development task.

docs.title: Do I need a Proxmox server to use this?
docs.text:
No! The default mock mode works completely standalone with no Proxmox server required. It's perfect for:

  • Development and testing
  • CI/CD pipelines
  • Learning the Proxmox API
  • Building integrations before deploying

If you want to connect to a real Proxmox server, switch to real mode by setting PROXMOX_API_MODE=real.

docs.title: What Proxmox VE version is supported?
docs.text:
The current schema is based on Proxmox VE 8.1, but it should work with:

  • ✅ Proxmox VE 8.x (fully supported)
  • ✅ Proxmox VE 7.x (mostly compatible)
  • ⚠️ Proxmox VE 6.x (some endpoints may differ)

You can regenerate schemas for different versions using the code generation pipeline.

docs.title: Is this an official Proxmox project?
docs.text:
No. This is an independent, community-driven project. It's not affiliated with or endorsed by Proxmox Server Solutions GmbH.

The project uses publicly available Proxmox VE API documentation and generates OpenAPI schemas from live API exploration.

docs.title: Can I use this in production?
docs.text:
Yes, but with considerations:

  • Mock mode: Only for development/testing (data is in-memory and not persistent)
  • Real mode: Yes, but it adds a validation layer between clients and Proxmox. Consider:
  • Additional latency (~20-100ms)
  • Single point of failure (if the proxy goes down)
  • Security: The proxy needs Proxmox credentials

For production, consider whether you need the validation/proxy layer, or if direct Proxmox API access is better for your use case.

docs.title: How do I install it?
docs.text:
# Using pip
pip install proxmox-openapi

# Using uv (recommended)
uv pip install proxmox-openapi

# From source
git clone https://github.com/emersonfelipesp/proxmox-openapi.git
cd proxmox-openapi
uv pip install -e .

See the Installation Guide for more details.

docs.title: How do I run the server?
docs.text:
# Default (mock mode)
uvicorn proxmox_openapi.main:app --reload

# Real mode
export PROXMOX_API_MODE=real
export PROXMOX_URL=https://proxmox.example.com:8006
export PROXMOX_API_TOKEN=PVEAPIToken=user@realm!tokenid=uuid
uvicorn proxmox_openapi.main:app --reload

See the Quick Start Guide for more details.

docs.title: What are the system requirements?
docs.text:
• Python: 3.11 or later (required)
  • Memory: ~100MB (mock) or ~80MB (real)
  • Disk: ~50MB installed
  • OS: Linux, macOS, Windows (any platform with Python support)

docs.title: What is mock mode?
docs.text:
Mock mode provides an in-memory simulation of the Proxmox VE API. It:

  • Loads 646 pre-generated endpoints from OpenAPI schema
  • Provides full CRUD operations (Create, Read, Update, Delete)
  • Stores data in memory (resets on restart)
  • Requires no Proxmox server

Perfect for development, testing, and CI/CD pipelines.

docs.title: Can I customize the mock data?
docs.text:
Yes! You can load custom test data from JSON or YAML files:

export PROXMOX_MOCK_DATA_PATH=/path/to/custom-data.json
uvicorn proxmox_openapi.main:app --reload

See the Mock API Guide for data format examples.

docs.title: Does mock data persist across restarts?
docs.text:
No. Mock mode uses in-memory storage, so all data is lost when the server stops.

If you need persistent mock data: - Use custom mock data files (loaded on startup) - Consider contributing a database-backed mode (future feature)

docs.title: Can I use mock mode in CI/CD?
docs.text:
Absolutely! Mock mode is perfect for CI/CD:

# Example GitHub Actions workflow
- name: Start Proxmox Mock API
  run: |
    uvicorn proxmox_openapi.main:app &
    sleep 2  # Wait for startup

- name: Run integration tests
  run: pytest tests/integration/

No external dependencies, fast startup, predictable behavior.

docs.title: How do I connect to a real Proxmox server?
docs.text:
Set these environment variables:

export PROXMOX_API_MODE=real
export PROXMOX_URL=https://proxmox.example.com:8006
export PROXMOX_API_TOKEN=PVEAPIToken=user@realm!tokenid=uuid  # Recommended

# OR use username/password
export PROXMOX_USERNAME=root@pam
export PROXMOX_PASSWORD=your-password

Then start the server:

uvicorn proxmox_openapi.main:app --reload

See the Real API Guide for full details.

docs.title: Should I use API tokens or username/password?
docs.text:
Use API tokens (recommended):

  • More secure (scoped permissions)
  • No password exposure
  • Can be revoked individually
  • Auditable

Username/password is provided as a fallback for legacy setups.

docs.title: How do I disable SSL verification?
docs.text:
export PROXMOX_API_VERIFY_SSL=false

Warning: Only use this for development/testing with self-signed certificates. Never disable SSL verification in production.

docs.title: What happens if the Proxmox server is down?
docs.text:
The proxy will return a 503 Service Unavailable error when it cannot reach the Proxmox server.

Your application should handle this gracefully with retry logic or fallback behavior.

docs.title: Does real mode cache responses?
docs.text:
No. Every request is forwarded to the Proxmox API in real-time. This ensures you always get the latest data but adds latency.

Future versions may add optional caching for read-only operations.

docs.title: How do I authenticate with the API?
docs.text:
It depends on the mode:

Mock Mode (Default): - No authentication required by default - You can add custom auth middleware if needed

Real Mode: - Automatically authenticates with Proxmox using your configured credentials - Clients just call the proxy endpoints (no need to handle Proxmox auth)

docs.title: Where is the API documentation?
docs.text:
Interactive Swagger UI is available at:

http://localhost:8000/docs

Alternative ReDoc interface:

http://localhost:8000/redoc

Raw OpenAPI JSON schema:

http://localhost:8000/openapi.json

docs.title: How do I create a VM?
docs.text:
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/nodes/pve1/qemu \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "vmid": 100,
    "name": "test-vm",
    "memory": 4096,
    "cores": 2,
    "sockets": 1,
    "scsi0": "local-lvm:32",
    "net0": "virtio,bridge=vmbr0"
  }'

See the API Reference for all available endpoints.

docs.title: What's the difference between this and pynetbox?
docs.text:
pynetbox is a Python client for NetBox (DCIM/IPAM tool).

proxmox-openapi is a FastAPI server for Proxmox VE (virtualization platform).

They're completely different projects for different tools. You might use both if you're managing infrastructure with both NetBox and Proxmox.

Yes! Contributions are welcome:

  • 🐛 Bug reports and fixes
  • ✨ New features

  • 🧪 Test coverage
  • 🎨 Code quality improvements

See the Development Guide for contribution guidelines.

Use the built-in code generation pipeline:

from proxmox_openapi.proxmox_codegen import ProxmoxCodegenPipeline

pipeline = ProxmoxCodegenPipeline(

    output_dir="./output",

This generates: - raw_capture.json - Crawler output - openapi.json - OpenAPI 3.1 schema - pydantic_models.py - Pydantic models

See the Development Guide for details.

docs.title: Server won't start - "Module not found" error
docs.text:
Make sure you've installed the package:

uv pip install proxmox-openapi

# Or for development
uv pip install -e .

docs.title: Mock mode: "Schema not found" error
docs.text:
The pre-generated schema should be included in the package. If missing:

# Reinstall the package
uv pip install --force-reinstall proxmox-openapi

docs.title: Real mode: "Connection refused" error
docs.text: Check that: 1. PROXMOX_URL is correct (including https:// and port :8006) 2. Proxmox server is running and accessible 3. Firewall allows access on port 8006 4. SSL certificate is valid (or set PROXMOX_API_VERIFY_SSL=false for testing)

docs.title: Real mode: "401 Unauthorized" error
docs.text:
Check your credentials:

# For API token
echo $PROXMOX_API_TOKEN  # Should be: PVEAPIToken=user@realm!tokenid=uuid

# For username/password
echo $PROXMOX_USERNAME  # Should be: user@realm (e.g., root@pam)
echo $PROXMOX_PASSWORD

Verify credentials work with direct Proxmox API access:

curl -k https://proxmox.example.com:8006/api2/json/version \
  -H "Authorization: PVEAPIToken=user@realm!tokenid=uuid"

docs.title: Real mode: "SSL certificate verification failed"
docs.text:
For development/testing with self-signed certificates:

export PROXMOX_API_VERIFY_SSL=false

Never disable SSL verification in production. Instead, add the Proxmox CA certificate to your system trust store.

docs.title: Slow startup time
docs.text:
Normal startup times: - Mock mode: ~1 second (loads 5.2MB schema) - Real mode: ~500ms (minimal initialization)

If startup is significantly slower: 1. Check disk I/O performance 2. Ensure you're not in debug mode with hot-reload (use --reload only for development) 3. Verify Python version (3.11+ recommended for best performance)

docs.title: High memory usage
docs.text:
Expected memory usage: - Mock mode: ~100MB (schema + in-memory state) - Real mode: ~80MB (schema + aiohttp session)

If memory usage is significantly higher: 1. Check for memory leaks in custom code 2. Monitor the size of mock state (large CRUD operations) 3. Consider restarting periodically in development

docs.title: Endpoints return different data than real Proxmox
docs.text:
Mock mode: Returns simulated data, not real Proxmox data. Use custom mock data to match your test scenarios.

Real mode: Should match Proxmox exactly. If not, check: 1. Proxmox version compatibility 2. Schema version matches your Proxmox installation 3. Report as a bug if validation is incorrect

docs.title: How many requests can it handle?
docs.text:
Mock mode: 10,000+ requests/second (limited by FastAPI/uvicorn)

Real mode: Limited by your Proxmox server capacity. The proxy adds ~20-100ms overhead for validation.

For high-traffic production use, consider: - Running multiple instances behind a load balancer - Using a production ASGI server (gunicorn with uvicorn workers) - Direct Proxmox API access (bypassing the proxy)

docs.title: Can I scale horizontally?
docs.text:
Yes! The server is stateless (in real mode) or uses in-memory state (mock mode).

For real mode: - Run multiple instances behind a load balancer - Each instance independently proxies to Proxmox

For mock mode: - Each instance has its own in-memory state - Not suitable for shared mock environments (use a database-backed mode instead)

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.11 or higher
  • pip or uv package manager

docs.title: Install from PyPI
docs.text:
The easiest way to install proxmox-openapi:

pip install proxmox-openapi

Or using uv (recommended for faster installation):

uv pip install proxmox-openapi

docs.title: Install from Source
docs.text:
For development or to get the latest unreleased features:

# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/emersonfelipesp/proxmox-openapi.git
cd proxmox-openapi

# Install with uv (recommended)
uv sync

# Or with pip
pip install -e .

docs.title: Docker Installation
docs.text:
Run the pre-built Docker image:

# Pull the image
docker pull ghcr.io/emersonfelipesp/proxmox-openapi:latest

# Run in mock mode (default)
docker run -p 8000:8000 ghcr.io/emersonfelipesp/proxmox-openapi:latest

# Run in real mode
docker run -p 8000:8000 \
  -e PROXMOX_API_MODE=real \
  -e PROXMOX_API_URL=https://pve.example.com:8006 \
  -e PROXMOX_API_TOKEN_ID=user@pam!mytoken \
  -e PROXMOX_API_TOKEN_SECRET=your-secret \
  ghcr.io/emersonfelipesp/proxmox-openapi:latest

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
# Check installed version
python -c "import proxmox_openapi; print(proxmox_openapi.__version__)"

# Run the mock API
proxmox-openapi-mock

Visit http://localhost:8000/docs to confirm the API is running.

docs.title: Mock API Mode
docs.text: Complete guide to using proxmox-openapi in mock mode for development and testing.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Mock mode provides a fully functional Proxmox API simulation with:

  • ✅ 646 pre-generated endpoints from official Proxmox API
  • ✅ In-memory CRUD operations - Create, read, update, delete
  • ✅ Automatic data seeding - Sample data generated on first access
  • ✅ State persistence - Changes persist across requests (during runtime)
  • ✅ Custom mock data - Load your own test data from JSON/YAML

docs.title: Method 1: Dedicated Mock Server
docs.text:
proxmox-openapi-mock

This runs a standalone mock-only server.

docs.title: Method 2: Main App in Mock Mode (Default)
docs.text:
# Mock mode is the default
uvicorn proxmox_openapi.main:app

# Or explicitly set mock mode
export PROXMOX_API_MODE=mock
uvicorn proxmox_openapi.main:app

Both methods provide the same functionality.

docs.title: Automatic Seeding
docs.text:
When you make your first GET request to an endpoint, mock data is automatically generated:

import httpx

# First GET request - data doesn't exist yet
response = httpx.get("http://localhost:8000/api2/json/nodes")

# Mock data automatically created and returned:
# [{"node": "pve", "status": "online", ...}]

docs.title: State Persistence
docs.text:
Changes persist in memory during the server lifetime:

# Create a VM
httpx.post(
    "http://localhost:8000/api2/json/nodes/pve/qemu",
    json={"vmid": 100, "name": "test-vm"}
)

# Later requests return the same VM
response = httpx.get("http://localhost:8000/api2/json/nodes/pve/qemu/100")
print(response.json())  # Returns the VM we created

Memory Only

Mock data is stored in RAM and will be lost when the server restarts.

docs.title: Custom Mock Data
docs.text: Load your own test data to simulate specific scenarios.

docs.title: JSON Format
docs.text:
Create mock-data.json:

{
  "/api2/json/nodes": [
    {
      "node": "pve1",
      "status": "online",
      "cpu": 0.25,
      "maxcpu": 16,
      "mem": 8589934592,
      "maxmem": 68719476736
    },
    {
      "node": "pve2",
      "status": "online",
      "cpu": 0.50,
      "maxcpu": 32,
      "mem": 17179869184,
      "maxmem": 137438953472
    }
  ],
  "/api2/json/cluster/resources": [
    {"type": "node", "node": "pve1", "status": "online"},
    {"type": "node", "node": "pve2", "status": "online"},
    {"type": "qemu", "vmid": 100, "name": "prod-web-01", "node": "pve1"},
    {"type": "qemu", "vmid": 101, "name": "prod-db-01", "node": "pve2"}
  ]
}

docs.title: YAML Format
docs.text:
Create mock-data.yaml:

/api2/json/nodes:
  - node: pve1
    status: online
    cpu: 0.25
    maxcpu: 16
  - node: pve2
    status: online
    cpu: 0.50
    maxcpu: 32

/api2/json/cluster/resources:
  - type: node
    node: pve1
    status: online
  - type: qemu
    vmid: 100
    name: prod-web-01
    node: pve1

docs.title: Load Custom Data
docs.text:
Environment VariableDefault LocationDocker 
export PROXMOX_MOCK_DATA_PATH=./mock-data.json
proxmox-openapi-mock

Place your file at the default path:

sudo mkdir -p /etc/proxmox-openapi
sudo cp mock-data.json /etc/proxmox-openapi/
proxmox-openapi-mock

docker run -p 8000:8000 \
  -v $(pwd)/mock-data.json:/data/mock.json \
  -e PROXMOX_MOCK_DATA_PATH=/data/mock.json \
  ghcr.io/emersonfelipesp/proxmox-openapi:latest

docs.title: GET Requests
docs.text:
Collections (arrays):

# Returns array of items
response = httpx.get("http://localhost:8000/api2/json/nodes")
# [{"node": "pve", ...}]

Single Resources (objects):

# Returns single object
response = httpx.get("http://localhost:8000/api2/json/nodes/pve/status")
# {"uptime": 12345, "cpu": 0.5, ...}

docs.title: POST Requests
docs.text:
Create new items in collections:

response = httpx.post(
    "http://localhost:8000/api2/json/nodes/pve/qemu",
    json={
        "vmid": 100,
        "name": "test-vm",
        "memory": 2048,
        "cores": 2,
    }
)

# Item added to collection AND created as individual resource
# Now accessible at:
# - /api2/json/nodes/pve/qemu (in the collection)
# - /api2/json/nodes/pve/qemu/100 (individual resource)

docs.title: PUT Requests
docs.text:
Replace entire resource:

response = httpx.put(
    "http://localhost:8000/api2/json/nodes/pve/qemu/100",
    json={
        "vmid": 100,
        "name": "test-vm-renamed",
        "memory": 4096,  # Changed
        "cores": 4,      # Changed
    }
)

# Entire object replaced with new values

docs.title: PATCH Requests
docs.text:
Partial update (merge):

response = httpx.patch(
    "http://localhost:8000/api2/json/nodes/pve/qemu/100",
    json={"memory": 8192}
)

# Only 'memory' field updated, other fields unchanged

docs.title: DELETE Requests
docs.text:
Remove resources:

response = httpx.delete("http://localhost:8000/api2/json/nodes/pve/qemu/100")

# Resource removed from:
# - Individual endpoint (/api2/json/nodes/pve/qemu/100)
# - Parent collection (/api2/json/nodes/pve/qemu)

docs.title: Query Parameters
docs.text:
Mock endpoints support query parameter filtering:

# Get all VMs
all_vms = httpx.get("http://localhost:8000/api2/json/cluster/resources?type=qemu")

# Filter by status
running = httpx.get("http://localhost:8000/api2/json/cluster/resources?type=qemu&status=running")

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Configure mock mode behavior:

Variable Default Description PROXMOX_API_MODE mock Set to mock for mock mode PROXMOX_MOCK_SCHEMA_VERSION latest OpenAPI schema version to use PROXMOX_MOCK_DATA_PATH /etc/proxmox-openapi/mock-data.json Custom mock data file path HOST 0.0.0.0 Server host to bind PORT 8000 Server port

docs.title: Integration Testing
docs.text:
import httpx
import pytest

@pytest.fixture
def proxmox_client():
    return httpx.Client(base_url="http://localhost:8000/api2/json")

def test_create_vm(proxmox_client):
    response = proxmox_client.post(
        "/nodes/pve/qemu",
        json={"vmid": 100, "name": "test", "memory": 2048}
    )
    assert response.status_code == 200

    # Verify VM was created
    vm = proxmox_client.get("/nodes/pve/qemu/100")
    assert vm.json()["vmid"] == 100

docs.title: CI/CD Pipelines
docs.text:
# .github/workflows/test.yml
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Start Mock Proxmox API
        run: |
          pip install proxmox-openapi
          proxmox-openapi-mock &
          sleep 2

      - name: Run Integration Tests
        run: pytest tests/integration/

docs.title: Local Development
docs.text:
# Start mock API
proxmox-openapi-mock

# In another terminal, develop your application
python my_proxmox_app.py

Your application can make Proxmox API calls without needing a real cluster!

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
Mock Limitations

  • No real Proxmox logic - Doesn't validate Proxmox-specific business rules
  • Memory-only storage - Data lost on restart
  • Simplified state - Doesn't track complex resource relationships
  • No async operations - Real Proxmox tasks are immediate in mock mode

For production use or testing against real Proxmox behavior, use Real API Mode.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: Get up and running with proxmox-openapi in under 5 minutes.

docs.title: Step 1: Install
docs.text: pip install proxmox-openapi

docs.title: Step 2: Start the Mock API
docs.text:
proxmox-openapi-mock

You should see output like:

INFO:     Started server process [12345]
INFO:     Waiting for application startup.
INFO:     Application startup complete.
INFO:     Uvicorn running on http://0.0.0.0:8000

docs.title: Step 3: Explore the API
docs.text:
Open your browser to:

http://localhost:8000/docs

You'll see the Swagger UI with 646 Proxmox API endpoints ready to use!

Try these endpoints:

  • GET / - Root endpoint showing API info
  • GET /mode - See current mode and configuration
  • GET /api2/json/nodes - List Proxmox nodes (mock data)
  • GET /api2/json/version - Get Proxmox version info

docs.title: Step 4: Make Your First Request
docs.text:
Python (httpx)Python (requests)curlJavaScript (fetch) 
import httpx

# Get nodes list
response = httpx.get("http://localhost:8000/api2/json/nodes")
print(response.json())

import requests

response = requests.get("http://localhost:8000/api2/json/nodes")
print(response.json())

curl http://localhost:8000/api2/json/nodes

fetch('http://localhost:8000/api2/json/nodes')
  .then(res => res.json())
  .then(data => console.log(data))

docs.title: Understanding Mock Mode
docs.text:
Mock Mode Behavior

  • In-memory state: All data stored in RAM during runtime
  • Automatic seeding: First GET request creates sample data
  • State persistence: Changes persist across requests (until restart)
  • No real Proxmox needed: Perfect for development and testing

from proxmox_openapi.schema import load_proxmox_generated_openapi

docs.title: Real API Mode
docs.text: Connect proxmox-openapi to actual Proxmox servers with full request/response validation.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Real mode transforms proxmox-openapi into a validated proxy for your Proxmox API:

  • ✅ 646 endpoints route to real Proxmox server
  • ✅ Request validation - Pydantic models ensure correct request format
  • ✅ Response validation - Guarantees response data integrity
  • ✅ Multiple auth methods - API tokens or username/password
  • ✅ SSL control - Verify certificates or disable for self-signed
  • ✅ Production-ready - Async HTTP client with connection pooling

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Running Proxmox VE server (7.x or 8.x)
  • API credentials (token or username/password)
  • Network access to Proxmox server

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
# Set environment variables
export PROXMOX_API_MODE=real
export PROXMOX_API_URL=https://pve.example.com:8006
export PROXMOX_API_TOKEN_ID=user@pam!mytoken
export PROXMOX_API_TOKEN_SECRET=xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx

# Start the API
uvicorn proxmox_openapi.main:app

Now all requests to http://localhost:8000/api2/json/* are proxied to your real Proxmox server!

docs.title: Method 1: API Tokens (Recommended)
docs.text:
Create token in Proxmox:

  1. Log into Proxmox web UI
  2. Navigate to Datacenter → Permissions → API Tokens
  3. Click Add and create a new token
  4. Note the Token ID and Secret (shown once!)

Configure:

export PROXMOX_API_TOKEN_ID=user@pam!mytoken
export PROXMOX_API_TOKEN_SECRET=xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx

Format: - Token ID: {username}@{realm}!{token-name} - Examples: - root@pam!automation - admin@pve!backup-token - deploy@pam!ci-cd

docs.title: Method 2: Username/Password
docs.text:
Less Secure

Password auth is less secure than tokens. Use tokens when possible.

export PROXMOX_API_USERNAME=root@pam
export PROXMOX_API_PASSWORD=your-password

The client will automatically obtain a ticket on first request.

docs.title: Verify SSL (Default, Recommended)
docs.text:
export PROXMOX_API_VERIFY_SSL=true  # Default

Use this with: - Public CA-signed certificates - Properly configured domain certificates

docs.title: Disable SSL Verification
docs.text:
export PROXMOX_API_VERIFY_SSL=false

Use this for: - Self-signed certificates - Development/testing environments - Internal Proxmox clusters with private CAs

Production Warning

Disabling SSL verification in production is a security risk. Use proper certificates instead.

docs.title: Environment Variables Reference
docs.text: Variable Required Default Description PROXMOX_API_MODE Yes mock Must be real for real mode PROXMOX_API_URL Yes - Proxmox server URL PROXMOX_API_TOKEN_ID If using tokens - API token ID PROXMOX_API_TOKEN_SECRET If using tokens - API token secret PROXMOX_API_USERNAME If using password - Username (e.g., root@pam) PROXMOX_API_PASSWORD If using password - Password PROXMOX_API_VERIFY_SSL No true Verify SSL certificates HOST No 0.0.0.0 Server bind host PORT No 8000 Server bind port

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
import httpx

# All requests go to real Proxmox
base_url = "http://localhost:8000/api2/json"

# List nodes
nodes = httpx.get(f"{base_url}/nodes").json()
print(nodes)  # Real data from your Proxmox cluster!

# Get specific node status
status = httpx.get(f"{base_url}/nodes/pve/status").json()
print(f"Uptime: {status['uptime']} seconds")

docs.title: Create a VM
docs.text:
import httpx

response = httpx.post(
    "http://localhost:8000/api2/json/nodes/pve/qemu",
    json={
        "vmid": 100,
        "name": "test-vm",
        "memory": 2048,
        "cores": 2,
        "sockets": 1,
        "scsi0": "local-lvm:32",
        "ide2": "local:iso/debian-12.iso,media=cdrom",
        "net0": "virtio,bridge=vmbr0",
    }
)

if response.status_code == 200:
    print("VM created successfully!")
    print(response.json())
else:
    print(f"Error: {response.status_code}")
    print(response.json())

docs.title: List All VMs
docs.text:
import httpx

response = httpx.get("http://localhost:8000/api2/json/cluster/resources?type=vm")
vms = response.json()

for vm in vms:
    print(f"VM {vm['vmid']}: {vm['name']} (status: {vm['status']})")

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Request arrives at proxmox-openapi
  2. Pydantic validation checks request matches OpenAPI schema
  3. Validation passes → Forward to real Proxmox
  4. Proxmox responds with data
  5. Pydantic validation checks response matches schema
  6. Validation passes → Return to client

docs.title: Validation Benefits
docs.text:
• Type safety - Catch invalid requests before hitting Proxmox
  • Schema enforcement - Ensure API contract compliance
  • Error clarity - Detailed validation error messages
  • Documentation - Self-documenting via Pydantic models

docs.title: Example: Invalid Request
docs.text:
import httpx

# Missing required field 'name'
response = httpx.post(
    "http://localhost:8000/api2/json/nodes/pve/qemu",
    json={"vmid": 100, "memory": 2048}  # Missing 'name'
)

print(response.status_code)  # 422 Unprocessable Entity
print(response.json())
# {
#   "detail": [
#     {
#       "loc": ["body", "name"],
#       "msg": "field required",
#       "type": "value_error.missing"
#     }
#   ]
# }

docs.title: Connection Refused
docs.text:
Failed to connect to Proxmox API: Cannot connect to host pve.example.com:8006

Solutions: - Verify Proxmox server is running - Check network connectivity: ping pve.example.com - Verify port 8006 is accessible: telnet pve.example.com 8006 - Check firewall rules

docs.title: SSL Certificate Verification Failed
docs.text:
SSL: CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED

Solutions: - Add proper SSL certificate to Proxmox - Disable verification (dev only): PROXMOX_API_VERIFY_SSL=false - Install CA certificate on system running proxmox-openapi

docs.title: Authentication Failed
docs.text:
Proxmox authentication failed: 401 Unauthorized

Solutions: - Verify credentials are correct - Check token hasn't expired - Ensure user has required permissions in Proxmox - For tokens: verify token privilege separation is configured correctly

docs.title: Permission Denied
docs.text:
Proxmox API error: 403 Forbidden

Solutions: - Grant required permissions to user/token in Proxmox - Check Datacenter → Permissions in Proxmox UI - Common required privileges: - VM.Allocate - VM.Config.* - Datastore.AllocateSpace - Pool.Allocate (if using pools)

docs.title: Recommended Setup
docs.text:
# Use systemd service
sudo tee /etc/systemd/system/proxmox-openapi.service <<EOF
[Unit]
Description=Proxmox OpenAPI Gateway
After=network.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=proxmox-api
Group=proxmox-api
WorkingDirectory=/opt/proxmox-openapi
Environment="PROXMOX_API_MODE=real"
Environment="PROXMOX_API_URL=https://pve.example.com:8006"
Environment="PROXMOX_API_TOKEN_ID=automation@pam!gateway"
EnvironmentFile=/etc/proxmox-openapi/env
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/uvicorn proxmox_openapi.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000
Restart=always

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF

# Store secret in protected file
sudo mkdir -p /etc/proxmox-openapi
sudo tee /etc/proxmox-openapi/env <<EOF
PROXMOX_API_TOKEN_SECRET=xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx
EOF
sudo chmod 600 /etc/proxmox-openapi/env

# Enable and start
sudo systemctl enable proxmox-openapi
sudo systemctl start proxmox-openapi

docs.title: Behind Reverse Proxy (nginx)
docs.text:
server {
    listen 443 ssl http2;
    server_name proxmox-api.example.com;

    ssl_certificate /etc/ssl/certs/proxmox-api.crt;
    ssl_certificate_key /etc/ssl/private/proxmox-api.key;

    location / {
        proxy_pass http://localhost:8000;
        proxy_set_header Host $host;
        proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;
    }
}
